title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: The over priced liberal arts and private universities are the schools that gave huge scholarships. They ended being the lowest cost. So if you're a top student DO apply to those!
author: @anniek4919

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: Back in 2004 at age 24 University of Phoenix got me for three classes before I realized I could get the same education at a community college for a fraction of the price.
author: @malcorub

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: Correction to the private vs public schools point: private colleges often offer generous financial aid that can make it cheaper to attend than a state school. State schools offer offer almost no institutional aid since they don't have the large endowments of private and are already offering such a reduced tuition to begin with. If you are interested in a non profit accredited private college then go ahead and apply and see how much fin aid they offer you and what the conditions to receive it are. Don't write off private colleges as "too expensive" just by looking at the sticker price of cost of attendance because almost nobody besides the super rich actually pay that amount.
author: @first9299

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: I agree with everything Shane is saying. There are really some schools out there that are focused more on earning rather than to educate and give value to what they can equip their graduates with.

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: My daughter applied to 6 or 7 colleges and universities.   I assumed the state schools would be cheaper. I was incorrect.   The private college gave my daughter a competitive scholarship based on her top 5% achievements in high school.   Still insanely expensive but the college has a solid internship program which I believe is very important.

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body:
One sidebar as someone who has worked in the tech & software industry for over 25 years: one of the biggest shocks to me was discovering how over-rated many students of so called 'Ivy League' or prestigious schools actually are. And I am not saying this as someone who is bitter about not attending such a school. It was never in the cards for me. But I have to admit, that did surprise me. Schools like Stanford, Harvard, Yale, etc all carry tremendous prestige. But honestly, I can't think of a single person I ever worked with who graduated from any of those schools that impressed me in the workplace. Harvard MBAs are in my opinion, the most over-rated. Generally speaking, I think that degree is better suited for people that want to work on Wall Street. But the mindset doesn't mesh well with the corporate sector. Most of those people only focus on the numbers, but don't have the slightest idea how to actually run a company. And their decisions are often predicated on what is best for them, as opposed to what is best for the company.
I will say that there is one prestigious school that has graduates that I have always admired and enjoyed working with; and that would be MIT. I've had several colleagues who graduated from that school and not only were they exceptionally smart, they were also very amicable and humble. I think the big difference is it is a school that really just focuses on merit and seems to have a positive vibe associated with it. That translates into graduates who are personable and are more in it for their love of technology and science.
author: @Diomedes01

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: The point about finding a college that supports you is spot on.  Not just for finding a career afterward, but guiding you through your academics for the time you're attending.  Ask current students; how is your academic advisor?  Are they proactive in catching problems before they get too bad?  Regular scheduled meetings during the school year?  Is there tutoring and essay writing assistance located in the library?  Some schools just leave you to fend for yourself; they graduate students who swim and discard those who sink.
author: @bridgecross

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body:
The one about schools with 'bad reputations' is a little bit sketchy. Often schools that aren't well liked only become unpopular through tribalism politics or rumors that grew too large for the truth to be heard. Speaking from personal experience, I ended up going to a school that was thought of as a school for the slower students because many people chastised it for a) offering dumb degrees and b) having a high acceptance rate. When I did some digging I found out that the employment rate for my degree was high, that students went on to do their Masters in good schools and that the actual difficulty of the degree was no different than any other degree in my country. The school did offer a few off shoot wonky degrees, and while the acceptance rate was fairly above average, it wasn't as high as its reputation implied. The drop out rate was considerably high for first year STEM students though.

Reputations are often misleading.
author: @thereccher8746

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: I 've been hearing and reading bits and pieces of information about these online for profit schools and you just confirmed it! I highly recommend anyone who are about to go to college to watch your videos They are so educational
author: @jeffreythechefgo714

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: I'm 26 years old and will be attending a private university in Austin, Texas this fall semester! (I'm really excited after returning back to my home city from new student orientation). I avoided college until mainly because I lacked the structure from not understanding what I'd like to do as a career. Honestly I wasn't aware of the way private universities work (or at least the school where I'm a student at). I just avoided private schools due to the cost. It blows my mind about how opposite my personal case worked out! Summary: 50,000 a year. I received federal grants, 3 school grants, then applied for the 2 small loans my fafsa could offer. My total per semester as a full time on campus student with meal plan is five thousand a semester. That's it. I can more than work with only 10K a year if it means I get to be a part of that campus I've already found my belonging with. My lack of research could have made this happen years earlier ago for me, but once admissions got into contact with me I've gained many big new insights. That is how I found this video because I'm so interesting in learning more about this new concept in my life lol
author: @BrookePainter

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: I am Puerto Rican so all universities tuition, whether they are public or private, cost more than 30,000 for four years, and the benefit of private ones is that they offer way more scholarship than a public ones

author: @thespiderexterminator2683

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body:
One big caveat about graduation rates, they are extremely dependent upon the financial status of the student. If you were working 20 plus hours a week, it is far more difficult to graduate in 4 years than someone who does not have to do this.

This is why I find it ridiculous that many private schools, where huge minorities of students don't take out loans, meaning that their parents are at minimum well off, have such high graduation rates. Their inputs are far wealthier here, and likely to be better educated than at regional state schools whose students are often much poorer, dependent upon loans and other jobs, may have to take time off or take a part-time schedule, etc just to be able to accommodate other responsibilities.
author: @whenhen

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: The "I got hazed so I get to be the one who hazes" is a poor excuse for passing on generational cruelty.
author: @lazygardens

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: I can't imagine being a grown ass man, 40 years old, still going back to campus to haze pledges their childrens age.
author: @Lexlugr

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: This video is like a memorial for all who've lost their lives in these nonsense processes. God bless you for that
author: @LadyChriss

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: Getting yo’ ass beat to be a part of an organization is such a disgrace to the ancestors given the harsh history of the diaspora. That’s crazy AF. Shoutout to you for the transparency.
author: @shaniceaj

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: It’s not just the D9 orgs! They’re hazing you to be a part of the band, welcome you to the freshman dorm, honor societies, etc….especially at HBCUs!!
author: @kimberlyb6522

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body:
Getting beat is crazy. I'm a Howard graduate and my roommate was a Delta. She didn't like me but kept encouraging me to pledge Delta. I felt it was a setup and she wanted me on line so she could beat my ahh. I never understood the concept or the brutality then turn around and call me sister. No I'll pass. I'm Me Phi Me. The risks don't out way the benefits.

I applied for a job once and the interviewer was a member of the same sorority. At the end of the interview she asked me if I was Greek. I told her no I'm African American. She said that's too bad because I was going to hire you. Please that has never stopped me from getting a job.

I have a sister in law who pledged doesn't like most of her sorors and the feeling is mutual...they are fake friendly.

This madness must stop. This is the same brutality our ancestors endured. Now we are perpetuating the same slave master behaviors.
author: @joanjones3677

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: I can serve my community without getting my A$$ kicked and humiliated by men claiming they’re my brothers. I wised up real quick as a young 20 y/o.
author: @1timdog1066

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: The irony of going to college to find a way out of a drug infested, abusive, and gang inherited environment. To go to college be arrested from the same thing you tried to leave...
author: @thewells3308

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: "how do you earn your spot?" Idk maybe have pledgees create programs, initiatives, campaigns etc successfully. Fundraising efforts to a certain amount, organize a social, volunteer, or networking event before the semester is over..there are other ways to earn your spot.
author: @gsignaturemessage

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: None of my family went to college until my generation. When my cousin thought of joining a frat and learned about hazing and hell week the first thing he said to us was “I could have just joined a gang back home, at least you just get jumped in the 1 time” 😂
author: @KarmaticallyKorrect

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: My cousin’s extremely handsome and the Alphas invited him, so he went. Those Alphas tried to SA him, but thank god he was able to fight his way out because he had MMA training. My cousin had bruises and those Alphas tried to press charges against him but thankfully the courts ruled in his favor. My cousin went to Harvard Med after that! He’s a doc of internal medicine. 🙏🏽
author: @user-bx2wl7we5k

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body:
The Ghost frat guy was clearly dealing with survivorship bias. He could acknowledge the harm and abuse that he suffered and caused but he couldn't take the further step to say that it was unnecessary, because that would require him to let go of the notion that he's "special" or "strong" for making it through.

Montel Jordan's interview was essentially bragging about having been put through the "old way" of hazing, as if it denotes some kind of merit within him.

I'm not really knocking either of those brothers but I'm trying to point out how we perpetuate cycles of harm by saying "I went through it, and look how well I turned out!" instead of saying "nobody ahould be abused like that, and I'm so sorry we haven't broken those chains yet".
author: @cuterpooter

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: Being socially awkward, uncool, and probably on the spectrum has its advantages. Being immune to cult tactics is one of them. Fraternities always weirded me out. Even groups of two can be a problem. Large groups of people dressing the same triggers my spidey sense that I need to avoid that group of people.
author: @James_St._James

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body:
Had a friend pass at ECU when she was online for Delta. She was a passenger with other pledges driving to a pledging related hair appointment. The driver was over tired from weeks of hazing and sleep deprivation she fell asleep and crashed killing my friend and another.... Victoria was the sweetest person you'd ever meet. Miss ya homie.

Wrote that before he got to that incident... Preciate you shining light on this
author: @charlesedwards3577

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: During my sophomore year in college, I was encouraged to join Omega and Alpha by close friends.  After learning what they had to endure, I knew it was not for me. I'm not a submissive individual  and would react badly to abuse.
author: @michael18044

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: There are tons of ways to build comradery without hazing. Miss me with all that "how do we do it, without hazing?" You spend time together, play sports, have cookouts, play games, go on trips etc.
author: @crystaljackson1797

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body:
Thank you for sharing this. My friend pledged Kappa back in the 90s. They were punching the guys in the face continuously and broke his jaw. He didn't want to go to the health center or call his family. We called his mom because he couldn't eat, and his roommate said he was struggling to breath at night from his jaw swelling. He was mad at us but later forgave us for telling his family. He actually had to have surgery to fix the damage they did.
I never understood how hazing fostered brotherhood or sisterhood.
author: @katrinaj7173

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: Wanted to pledge so badly during my college years . At that time I was about 120 pounds, small frame , and I knew I was gay and was not the most masculine dude. I knew I’d be targeted just by default. Fast forward to 47 years old , I’ve found 500 other ways in life to make life long friends, networking , and contributions to society without pledging.
author: @coreypatrick7230

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: My dad crossed in the late 70s and almost died as a result he had a cracked sternum from hell night and went into cardiac arrest. I’ve read the ritual book of omega psi phi … hazing is written within the process. All D9 and other Greek orgs are an offshoot of the Freemasons. So sad.
author: @sashanick9533

title: Hazing in Black Fraternities: The Real Story
body: My wife (an abuse survivor) and I would like to give the highest of praise for your bravery in talking about this subject openly. We especially want to acknowledge your bravery and profound integrity in not shying away from the fact that you (unfortunately) participated in the hazing that occurred at your fraternity. We cannot praise you enough for admitting to your participation. We have actually been watching you for a little while because of your insights and diligence in providing facts support your insights. On a personal note, as an old black man, I have been overjoyed to find a young black man with your level of integrity on Youtube. Also, my mother and sister were members of Alpha Kappa Alpha and I sincerely want to thank you for giving me more insight into the (possible) experiences that shaped their lives. They have both passed at this point so I don't have the opportunity to discuss this subject with them personally so I greatly appreciate you sharing as openly as you have. In closing let us just say that we know you will get backlash for this and we want to say that you absolutely did the right thing and we believe that the good people of the world appreciate you and we encourage you to continue with your work. Peace be with you.
author: @nicodemusprophiet4680

title: Southern New Hampshire University Review | Any Good for Busy Adults?
body: I'm currently a graduate student at SNHU and I went to college in NH for undergrad as well. They have a brick and mortar campus that I remember visiting in undergraduate, and they've made it accessible for someone like me who is going back to school in their late 30s by having an online program. I'm personally glad I chose SNHU for graduate school and plan on moving forward to a PhD. program after (hopefully). It's a wonderful school.
author: @StephanieGould

title: Southern New Hampshire University Review | Any Good for Busy Adults?
body: I just graduated from SNHU with my Masters in Cybersecurity (Class of 2025). I’m an immigrant from Zambia and SNHU staff made the process of translating my bachelor’s grades and credentials pretty smooth! I don’t have a background in technology so I’m transitioning into a new career. I attended my first cybersecurity interview before my graduation and the interviewers were pleased with the lab work and reporting we did in classes. Two weeks later I got the job offer and it’s really good pay🙌🏿 If I had to start over, I’d pick SNHU again.
author: @Kal-n2i8m

title: Southern New Hampshire University Review | Any Good for Busy Adults?
body: I was looking at schools and SNHU met my criteria for a campus, non-profit,  accredited, and a robust online class structure. The academic and onboarding process was painless and I love my advisors. I found the teachers approachable and really helpful, they wanted to see me succeed.

title: Southern New Hampshire University Review | Any Good for Busy Adults?

title: Southern New Hampshire University Review | Any Good for Busy Adults?
body: I just registered to SNHU first term, their $320 tuition per credit hour is really worth it. They have very active and excited academic councellor. Then you can draft your work before submitting for unlimited times, then they have 24/7 tutoring for unlimited times too. If you take online tutoring such as wyzant, they will easily charged $40-50 per hour. Then you have academic coach, peer coaching, and  plenty of webinar too. So I conclude their tuition already the cheapest per credit hour; plus all of their academic support, then SNHU (private, bigger institution) is one of the best hackable college, besides UMPI (public, smaller one). I registered to both to test them out.
author: @ishady27

title: Southern New Hampshire University Review | Any Good for Busy Adults?

title: Southern New Hampshire University Review | Any Good for Busy Adults?
body: You are correct that the instructors do not create any of the course content. They just grade and provide feedback. They are adjuncts paid very little, so they likely don’t have much time to connect on a personal level, though I’m sure some go above and beyond.

title: if USC were honest #usc #college #collegeadmissions #collegestudytips #ucla
body:
Hey!

I’m a graduate from the 90s!!

It was still a prestigious school, even back then.

I believe USC was ranked 51st in the nation out of 1,500 colleges when I graduated…

Just not in the top 25 or so like today.

Unless you use Forbes where I think USC was 14 last year?

They’ve changed those rankings and downgraded them unfortunately so the rankings are a little diluted.

But ranking isn’t everything… I had some of the best times of my life while there!

title: The TRUTH About USC… #usc #college #collegelife #university
body:
I was middle class and got accepted into USC…

Half my school was scholarships, the other half loans.

I had a full time job while going to school there.

In fact, all my close friends I met there were also middle class…

Everyone being rich who goes there isn’t true.

But, my maid did go to UCLA!

✌🏻

title: Touring Every Florida College So You Don't Have To | (UF UM UCF FAU ETC)
body: UCF didn't accept me (I applied a bit late in the year for the term I wanted), so I went with FAU instead. And oh man am I glad I went with FAU over UCF - the campus is much prettier, everyone there is really friendly, the buildings are really modern (and they're still renovating the old buildings), I was able to get an on-campus job that I really enjoyed (only a 10-minute commute!), and everything that I could ever need is on campus. It may not have a ton of things to do directly on campus (their events calendar is a bit lacking) but given all the positives I'm really proud to be an Owl! 🦉
author: @totallymarc

title: Touring Every Florida College So You Don't Have To | (UF UM UCF FAU ETC)
body: As a high school senior, who is currently looking Florida colleges, I toured UF and wasn’t as impressed by it as a lot of people are. The campus wasn’t really my vibe and everything felt very serious. I actually much preferred USF and UCF campuses and environments so much so that I didn’t end up applying to UF, but it was really interesting to see your takes on the campuses!
author: @bubbleowl1539

title: Touring Every Florida College So You Don't Have To | (UF UM UCF FAU ETC)
body:
UCF 2011 grad here who makes it back all the time for football games. Props on making it to all the campuses! As someone who has spent quite a bit of time at all of the below campuses, here’s my totally biased opinions for those looking to make the decision:

For UCF - location is incredible as you have your own college town but it’s a 15 min drive from downtown Orlando which has a SUPER underrated nightlife and is the fastest growing city in the country. Many of my college friends have jobs and continue to live in and love Orlando. It was definitely a party school when I was there and I had a blast, that said even when I went it was getting difficult to get into and more difficult now so I wonder if that’s changing. The uni identity/culture is very strong and the school is absolutely massive so whatever you’re into you can find like minded individuals. People who want an intimate college experience can get lost in its size. Cocoa beach isn’t too far, I used to go for a morning surf sesh before class. I think the campus is pretty but to each their own. I studied engineering with a business minor btw, one was much more intense than the other. Take a guess….

FIU - I took a summer semester there, campus is comparable to UCF in terms of looks (more Miami/modern/tropical architecture) and also a big school. It definitely felt like a commuter school and lacked some identity. Art museum was cool. DEFINITELY NOT in the heart of Miami like you say, it’s pretty far out west of downtown esp considering that Miami traffic.

FAU - nice but also commuter school vibe for the most part. Most people I know that went there didn’t live on/around campus. Plus Boca’s too sleepy for me but once again to each their own. BTW the football stadium is tiny (30k) and you can’t see the water unless you’re a VIP in the tower, video’s not accurate on that . They do have an on campus stadium and play in the American though so not hating on them, just bad description.

UF - it’s the flagship school in the state, great academics if you can get in probably should go. My brother was at UCF when I was deciding so it’s not always as cut and dry as academics. Plus Gainesville is in the absolute middle of nowhere, country ass town…

FSU - kind of like UF but replace the academics with partying. Tally, despite being the state capital, is even more so than Gainesville in the absolute middle of nowhere, country ass town. You are soooooo far from anything remotely resembling a city.

Miami - unless your parents are multimillionaires or they’re offering you significant financial aide to make it comparable to a public school tuition, DO NOT bury yourself in debt to attend that school. ITS A QUARTER MILLION IN TUITION FOR 4 YEARS?!? Maybe for med school…. Private schools are a racket in any case when better academics can be had at 1/10th the cost at a public uni. Campus is in a fantastic location but that doesn’t even come close to justifying the price. Unless you’re going to Harvard/Stanford/ etc, don’t even consider the price of a private institution.

USF - as a biased Knight, no… just no.
author: @mattj6940

title: Touring Every Florida College So You Don't Have To | (UF UM UCF FAU ETC)
body: USF is in a shady area. But, at the same time, the campus is awesome, there’s a bunch of natural areas right outside of campus, and a lot of cool places on campus if you like nature. body: I picked FSU over UF because FSU was the first school we did and also was amazed to see hills.
author: @dalej42

title: Touring Every Florida College So You Don't Have To | (UF UM UCF FAU ETC)
body: Love being an Owl alumni! It’s a completely different campus than when I was there, there was no engineering building, they hadn’t built the gym and new pool until my senior year, and we still had to drive to some random high school stadium 30 minutes away because the stadium hadn’t been built. The rapid development of that campus has been amazing to watch, and I love getting tickets that have access to the full service bar IN THE STADIUM! How awesome is that?!
author: @Alex-cz4fk

title: Southern University-Baton Rouge Residence Halls
body: That's not the jones hall I stayed in and totty and shade was girls dorms built when I was there back in 2001 and boley was the freshman girls dorm... is Dunn still open? What about mayberry? I used to stay in Carroll too... they demolished the triangle when I was there... damn I miss SU... trying to get my sons to go there it's a family tradition for my family... damn near everyone in my family went to SU...
author: @TrackLifeMediaGroup

body:
🙂🙂 ফাইনালি জাতীয় বিশ্ববিদ্যালয় থেকে এসে প্রাইভেট ভার্সিটিতে পড়ার সুযোগ পেলাম।
